Sat: May 9, 2026 14:11 PDT — GSD-T v3.24.10 — CURRENT

→ Conversational mode (no command spawn)

This is an exploratory research question — let me think through it with you and then surface a recommendation, not spawn a workflow. I want to do real research before answering, so let me kick off parallel investigations.

Used ToolSearch
I'll spawn parallel research agents and pull the printingpress.dev source directly. Letting them run while I think.


Browsed the web, ran 2 agents
I have enough to give you a strong, opinionated answer. Let me think this through, and then deliver.

CLI-First Rearchitecture — Deep Research & Recommendation
TL;DR
Yes, partial rearchitecture is warranted, but not a rewrite. GSD-T already has ~110 deterministic helpers in bin/ and scripts/ — the bones are right. The leak is at the subagent layer: validation prompts (qa, red-team, design-verify) and execute/verify Steps 1–2 spend tens of thousands of tokens doing things a CLI could do in <100ms with zero tokens. The fix is a CLI-first preflight pattern: every spawn is preceded by deterministic CLI calls that produce a small JSON brief, and the LLM only reasons over the brief.

Estimated savings if fully wired: 30–60% fewer tokens per execute/verify cycle, 3–5× fewer rate-limit pauses, and higher quality because typecheck/lint/dead-code/security findings become ground truth rather than LLM-judged.

What Printing Press actually is (and isn't)
printingpress.dev by Marshall Van Horn is a CLI generator built on a real thesis: "CLIs win for agents with ~100× fewer tokens than MCP tool definitions." You give it an OpenAPI spec, a HAR capture, or just a website, and it emits a Go binary + Claude Code skill + MCP server bundle. Generated CLIs ship with a local SQLite mirror, full-text search, and compound commands that collapse N round-trips into one (e.g., gh-goat prs --mine --failing instead of 4 MCP calls).

For GSD-T: Printing Press is not a replacement architecture — it's a factory for the integration-CLI tier. Generate pp-github, pp-linear, pp-vercel, pp-anthropic-console once, and every command can call them instead of MCP. The MCP tool-spec overhead alone is 5–20k tokens per turn that has MCP servers loaded — pp-* CLIs only pay for --help on demand.

The deeper lesson is the design philosophy, not the tool itself:

Local SQLite mirror beats remote API call → GSD-T's .gsd-t/scan/ and the new graph indexer are exactly this pattern; we should lean harder.
Compound commands beat ten round trips → most validation subagents do 5–20 sub-operations the LLM could batch into one CLI call.
Agent-native CLI beats raw HTTP → JSON-stream output, exit-code semantics, deterministic schemas.
Where GSD-T is bleeding tokens (from the inventory)
Site    What happens now    What a CLI could do    Tokens saved per cycle
gsd-t-execute Step 1+1.5    LLM reads CLAUDE.md, progress.md, all contracts, integration-points, all domain task lists, runs graph queries via prompt    Single gsd-t brief --domain X --json produces a 1–2k JSON snapshot    ~15–30k
gsd-t-verify Step 2    LLM-led test discovery + run + contract coverage audit + gap analysis    gsd-t verify-gate --json runs typecheck + lint + tests + contract-coverage + dead-code in parallel, returns a 500-token summary    ~30–80k
qa-subagent    LLM "detects test suites, runs each, counts pass/fail" — 50% of its work is mechanical    gsd-t qa-probe emits {vitest:{p:120,f:0}, playwright:{p:42,f:1,failures:[...]}}. LLM only judges quality of remaining E2E specs    ~10–25k per spawn
red-team-subagent boundary fuzzing    LLM crafts null/empty/huge/SQLi/XSS inputs from scratch    pp-fuzz <endpoint> (custom Printing Press CLI) generates standard payloads; LLM only reasons about contract-specific attacks    ~15–40k per spawn
design-verify-subagent Step 0–0.5    LLM does element count reconciliation + label cross-check by reading both pages    gsd-t design-diff --figma <url> --built <url> --json returns structured deviation list    ~20–50k per spawn
gsd-t-scan    LLM walks tree, infers complexity/security/architecture issues    scc -f json . && lizard --csv && trivy fs --format json && gitleaks detect --report-format json → 1k JSON digest    ~30–50k per scan
Repo-wide rename in quick    LLM Reads N files, emits N Edits    sg run -p 'OldName' -r 'NewName' --update-all then LLM verifies    ~10–40k per rename
Search-before-Read in execute    LLM uses Grep, then Reads matched files in full    rg --json + sg --json=stream → structured hits, Read only the lines around them    ~5–20k per search
Aggregate per typical milestone: a single execute → test-sync → integrate → verify → complete-milestone chain currently burns roughly 400k–900k tokens (from your token-log). CLI preflight pattern should land that at 200k–500k, with no quality loss — quality should go up because typecheck/lint/security become non-negotiable.

The 8 CLIs that would add the most value (ranked by ROI)
#    CLI    Where it slots in    Why
1    ast-grep (sg)    gsd-t-quick rename path; gsd-t-execute structural edits    Cross-language structural rewrites. One command replaces N LLM file-edits. Free, mature.
2    biome + ruff (or project's existing typer/linter)    gsd-t-verify mandatory gate; pre-commit    10× faster than eslint/black/prettier; JSON output; deterministic. Already partially in stack rules — needs to be auto-invoked, not just documented.
3    knip / vulture / cargo-udeps    gsd-t-verify and gsd-t-scan    Dead-code finder. Would catch the kind of "unused export accumulating across milestones" debt that scans currently miss.
4    scc + lizard + trivy + gitleaks    gsd-t-scan opening move    Repo snapshot (LOC, complexity, CVEs, secrets) in <2s; LLM reasons over JSON digest.
5    GNU parallel (or mise run --jobs)    gsd-t-verify Step 2    Fan out typecheck + lint + tests + dead-code + secrets in parallel; 4× wall-clock reduction.
6    Printing Press (custom presses)    gsd-t-triage-and-merge, integration commands    Generate pp-github, pp-linear once. Every PR-status / issue-fetch call drops from MCP-tool-spec overhead to a 200-token CLI invocation.
7    codex CLI (or claude -p with cheaper model)    "Cheap second opinion" sub-tool inside Red Team    Diversity of failure modes — using a different model family to attack your own code finds bugs the same model misses. Cheap (o4-mini-class).
8    ripgrep --json + jq    Mandatory first step in any "find X" before Read    Already installed; just needs the convention "no Read without rg first, and pipe through jq to extract {path, line, match}" — never paste raw rg output.
Honorable mentions: comby for cross-language pattern rewrites, hyperfine for before/after benchmarks during refactors, stryker/mutmut for mutation testing on critical paths (gates milestone completion).

Should we rearchitect? My recommendation: CLI-Preflight Pattern, not rewrite
Don't tear down the spawn architecture — it's working. Add one new contract and one new helper that every command file uses:

The pattern
EVERY subagent spawn now follows:
  1. CLI Preflight (deterministic, JSON output, <2s)
     → produces .gsd-t/briefs/{spawn-id}.json
  2. Subagent reads brief, NOT the raw repo
  3. Subagent does only the LLM-shaped work
  4. CLI Postflight verifies subagent's claimed output
     (tests actually pass? files actually changed? contract coverage actually green?)
  5. Discrepancy between Postflight and subagent report = task failure
Concrete additions
bin/gsd-t-cli-preflight.cjs — wraps the 8 CLIs above. Single entry: gsd-t preflight --kind {execute|verify|qa|red-team|design-verify|scan} --domain X --json. Returns a JSON brief sized to the spawn type.
bin/gsd-t-verify-gate.cjs — the mandatory parallel gate: typecheck + lint + tests + dead-code + secrets + complexity. JSON in, JSON out. Becomes Step 2 of gsd-t-verify.
.gsd-t/contracts/cli-preflight-contract.md — schema, freshness rules, fail-open vs fail-closed semantics, allowed CLIs.
Update validation-subagent prompts (templates/prompts/*-subagent.md) to consume the brief first, with a hard rule: "If you're about to grep/read/run-test, check the brief — the answer is probably already there."
Printing Press integration tier — bin/gsd-t-pp/ directory hosting generated pp-github, pp-linear, etc. Add gsd-t doctor --pp-presses to verify they're built.
Stack rules execution — today most stack rules are prose the LLM reads. Convert the verifiable ones (TS strict mode, no any, React Query for server data, Playwright functional asserts) into lint rules (eslint plugin, custom ast-grep rules) so the verify gate enforces them mechanically. Stack rule violation = failed grep/lint, not LLM judgment.
What NOT to rewrite
The always-headless spawn model — perfect.
The Task subagent + token capture wrapper pattern — the JSON brief flows through the same captureSpawn plumbing.
The command file structure — they get shorter as more steps move to preflight CLIs, not restructured.
MCP servers — keep them for low-frequency, exploratory operations. Replace MCP only for high-frequency calls where Printing Press CLIs win.
Risks and tradeoffs
CLI versioning hell — biome 2.x JSON differs from 1.x. Mitigation: pin via mise or package.json, add to gsd-t doctor.
Cross-platform — Windows users, brew vs apt. Most of the top 8 ship pre-built binaries; document install in gsd-t doctor --install-clis.
Brief staleness — file changes after preflight invalidate the brief. Mitigation: hash-stamp briefs, regenerate if mtime advanced.
Over-determination risk — if the brief says "no issues," the LLM might rubber-stamp. Mitigation: Red Team must include "challenge the preflight brief" as an attack vector.
Custom Printing Press CLIs are debt — every press needs maintenance when the source API drifts. Start with 2–3 highest-frequency integrations only.
Suggested next step
If you want this to land: spawn a focused M55 (or whatever the next slot is) with the scope "CLI-Preflight Pattern + Verify Gate" — build bin/gsd-t-cli-preflight.cjs + bin/gsd-t-verify-gate.cjs, write the contract, wire them into gsd-t-execute Step 1 and gsd-t-verify Step 2, measure token delta on the next 3 milestones. If the delta is ≥30%, expand to validation subagents in M56. Save Printing Press integration tier for M57 once you've proven the pattern.

Want me to draft the milestone scope and partition it now?

Sources
Printing Press homepage
cli-printing-press repo
Printing Press library
Claude Code CLI reference
ast-grep · comby · ripgrep
scc · lizard · trivy · gitleaks · knip
just · mise · hyperfine
opencode · codex · aider · goose
We have run into rate limit failures for parallel processing of 3 or more processes spawned in parallel.  These are Claude undocumented rate limits that we only run into when we spawn too many parallel processes.  This becomes a big blocker on speeding up the development workflow.  Can CLI's help to Increase parallelism capacity.

Sat: May 9, 2026 14:29 PDT — GSD-T v3.24.10 — CURRENT

→ Conversational mode (no command spawn)

Short answer: yes, dramatically. Your rate-limit ceiling isn't really a parallelism limit — it's a Claude-token-rate limit. Every parallel worker that's an LLM counts against the same per-account ITPM/OTPM bucket. Workers that are CLIs don't. So the lever isn't "spawn fewer workers" — it's "make most workers stop being LLMs."

Let me think through this properly.

What you're actually hitting
Claude has three undocumented-but-real per-account caps that kick in well before the published ones:

Concurrent in-flight requests (~3–5 for Pro/Max on a single org/key)
Input tokens per minute (ITPM) — the silent killer; 3 parallel workers each pulling 80k context = 240k ITPM, which trips the cap on the second turn even if the first turn was fine
Output tokens per minute (OTPM) — less common, but Red Team + 2 workers writing diffs at the same time can spike
The "3 parallel processes" wall is almost certainly ITPM, not concurrency. Each headless worker re-loads CLAUDE.md + contracts + domain scope on its first read — that's 30–60k of input before it does any work, multiplied by N workers, in the same 60-second window.

Why CLIs change the math
A CLI worker is 0 ITPM, 0 OTPM, 0 concurrent slots. It only borrows the LLM at the end, when the orchestrator merges results. Three patterns matter:

Pattern 1 — CLI workers in parallel, LLM at the join
Today: Worker A (LLM) || Worker B (LLM) || Worker C (LLM) → 3 LLM slots, 3× ITPM.
Better: CLI-A || CLI-B || CLI-C → tiny JSON briefs → 1 LLM that judges the joined brief. You get 8-way or 16-way parallelism (limited by CPU, not Claude) with one LLM slot.

This is the right shape for verify, scan, qa-probe, dead-code, security, contract-coverage, lint, typecheck, test runs, Playwright execution, design-diff element counting. All of it.

Pattern 2 — CLI preflight shrinks each LLM worker's ITPM
Today: each parallel execute worker reads 60k of context to learn what to do.
Better: orchestrator runs gsd-t brief --domain X --json once per domain (deterministic, ~50ms, 0 tokens), writes to .gsd-t/briefs/X.json, and the worker reads only the brief (~2k). Three workers now consume ~6k ITPM total instead of 180k. You can run 8 workers under the same cap that's currently blocking 3.

Pattern 3 — Different model families to escape one bucket
Spawn 1 Claude worker + 1 codex exec + 1 gemini -p — three workers, three independent rate buckets. Useful for Red Team specifically (diversity is also a quality win). Not a panacea — auth/cost/quality vary — but for adversarial QA where you want model diversity, it doubles as a parallelism multiplier.

Concrete capacity uplift
Rough math for your verify gate today vs. CLI-fronted:

Today (3 LLM workers)    CLI-fronted (8 CLI workers + 1 LLM judge)
Concurrent Claude slots    3    1
Peak ITPM    ~240k    ~30k
Peak OTPM    ~30k    ~5k
Wall-clock    bottlenecked by slowest LLM    bottlenecked by slowest test suite
Rate-limit pauses per milestone    2–6    ~0
You'd go from "3 workers is the ceiling" to "the ceiling is whatever your CPU can run" — for the deterministic portion of the work.

What I'd actually wire
Three concrete things, in priority order:

bin/gsd-t-parallel-cli.cjs — a fan-out runner that takes a JSON job spec like [{cli:"biome",args:[...]},{cli:"tsc",args:[...]},...], runs them via GNU parallel or Node's worker pool, captures JSON output, returns a merged result. This is the new substrate for verify, scan, and the deterministic half of every validation subagent. Zero LLM tokens.
CLI-preflight + brief pattern (the M55 I sketched in the previous turn) — every LLM spawn reads a pre-built brief instead of the raw repo. This is the single biggest ITPM reduction available. Lets you stack 6–8 LLM workers under the cap that currently blocks 3.
Worker-tier escape valve — make the orchestrator aware of per-bucket limits and route the third+ concurrent LLM worker to codex exec or gemini -p only when it's a task where model diversity is fine (Red Team, doc summarization, second-opinion review). Don't use it for primary execute workers — quality matters more than parallelism there.
Gotchas
MCP tool-spec overhead is per-turn, per-worker. If your workers have heavy MCP servers loaded, every turn is paying 5–20k of tool-spec tax that counts against ITPM. Audit which MCP servers the worker actually needs and trim. Printing Press CLIs replace MCP for high-frequency calls and skip this tax entirely.
Streaming reads stil

**Mystery solved**: M55 was just defined at **2026-05-09 15:04** — a few minutes ago. That was the headless milestone child I spawned earlier. It ran for ~30s before I killed it, and in that window it managed to:

1. Read `progress.md`
2. Run `npm test` to baseline (2262/2262 + 1 documented env-bleed)
3. Write the M55 row + decision log entry
4. Set status to `M55 DEFINED`

But it **invented a different scope** for M55 because the prompt I gave it was just the title `"M55: CLI-Preflight Pattern + Parallel-CLI Substrate + Verify Gate"` — no charter context. The headless child plausibly inferred from naming + recent pain points (M48/M49 silent-skip regressions) what M55 should be. Its scope is *adjacent* but not the same as our Desktop alignment:

- **Existing M55**: state-precondition checks (branch, ports, deps, contracts) + parallel substrate + verify-gate consumption
- **Our Desktop M55**: token-saving CLI brief generator + parallel substrate + verify-gate as off-the-shelf-CLI fan-out

Both are useful. They share the parallel substrate. They share verify-gate. They diverge on what "preflight" means and on the rate-limit-probe / falsifiable token-reduction criteria.

I need your call here — this is a real fork in the road and not something I should resolve unilaterally:
What is the difference?  What do you suggest should be merged?

What would you like to clarify before I reformulate the question? A few angles I can elaborate on if useful:

- **The exact difference** between the two M55 scopes (what each "preflight" means, what each ships)
- **Whether the existing M55 was truly intentional** or an artifact of my earlier accidental headless spawn
- **What "merge" would actually look like** — domain count, file overlaps, success criteria
- **Whether the falsifiable rate-limit / 40%-token-reduction criteria can attach to the existing scope**
- Something else entirely

Tell me what's unclear and I'll dig in before posing the question again.

I agree with merge.  Just out of curiosity, why do you keep adding things like ~2-3 day execute window or Any effort estimates that usually sound like manual development rather than GSD-T development.  If there's truly no value in that, then please add to your memory that you don't need to add those numbers anymore.

Honest framing first: I don't have memory of that earlier research from last week — sessions don't carry forward unless I save the finding to my memory store, and I don't see anything matching that specific finding in `MEMORY.md`. So what I'll give you is a reasoned answer about *why* such a delay would happen, not a recall of what I told you before. Treat it as a fresh analysis.

## Why parallelism failures often emerge an hour later, not immediately

There are three rate-limit windows operating simultaneously on Claude accounts, and they interact differently with parallel workloads:

**1. Per-minute caps (ITPM, OTPM) — fire instantly**
If you genuinely exceed input-tokens-per-minute, you 429 within the first burst. This is the "hit the wall in 30 seconds" failure mode. Not what was happening in the "an hour later" reports.

**2. 5-hour rolling-window cap — fires later**
Pro and Max subscriptions have a rolling-window quota measured over **5 hours of activity**, not per-minute. The mechanics:

- The window starts ticking from your **first message** in a session.
- The cap is a budget of "Claude-equivalent units" you can consume across that 5-hour window.
- A small parallel burst doesn't trip it; sustained parallelism does.

If you run 3 parallel workers continuously, you're consuming the budget 3× faster than serial. A budget that would normally last 5 hours of solo work gets exhausted in ~1h 40m of 3-way parallelism. **That's why the failure shows up "an hour or more later" — you're not hitting a parallelism limit, you're hitting a cumulative-consumption limit accelerated by parallelism.**

**3. Concurrent-request cap — fires immediately, but only at high N**
The "10 simultaneous Opus requests" thing we just tested. This is a per-second concurrency ceiling, not a token budget. It fires within 3 seconds of the burst.

## Which one was probably the "hour later" report?

Almost certainly **the 5-hour rolling window**, with parallelism acting as a multiplier on consumption rate. The pattern people see:

- Hour 0: start parallel work, everything fine
- Hour 0:30 to 1:00: still working, no errors, productivity feels great
- Hour 1:00–1:30: first 429s start appearing, intermittent at first
- Hour 1:30+: nearly every request 429s, no recovery until the rolling window slides forward

The reason it feels mysterious is that nothing **changed** between minute 5 and minute 65 — same workers, same parallelism, same prompts. But the budget has been depleting the whole time. The 429 fires at the threshold, not at the moment work began.

## What this means for your M55 measurement

Our probes are testing *concurrent-request ceiling* (cap #3 above) — instant failure mode. They're not testing the *cumulative-budget exhaustion* (cap #2) that causes the "hour later" failures.

The cap that would cause M55's pain in actual milestone work isn't N=10 simultaneous workers; it's "5 hours of running 6 parallel workers each consuming 60k of cached context per turn." That's a totally different math. A clean Opus result of "no cliff to N=50 in a 7-minute probe" wouldn't disprove the hour-later failure pattern — it would just mean the concurrency wasn't the problem.

If you want to actually falsify the hour-later cliff, you'd need a sustained-load probe: spawn N=6 workers, keep them busy for 90+ minutes by feeding them tasks back-to-back, and watch when the first 429 appears. That's a different (and much more expensive) test than what's running now.

Want me to add that to the SC5 measurement spec, or is the concurrent-cap data enough to ship M55?

→ Conversational mode (architecture clarification)

Two different things are getting conflated, and the distinction matters:

## Context window vs. plan usage limit

**Context window** = the in-memory token budget *of a single Claude session*. This conversation's context. M55 was supposed to make individual workers use less of THIS by replacing 30–60k repo re-reads with 2k briefs. That part is real and shipped.

**Plan usage limit** = the rolling-window quota *across your entire Claude Max subscription*, summed over every session, every detached worker, every probe spawn — for the past 5 hours. M55 doesn't help here directly. If anything, M55's tooling spawned MORE workers than usual today (the probes), which consumed MORE of your subscription budget.

So: **context window** is a per-session container; **plan usage** is a per-subscription-per-window meter. M55 attacks the first; what you just hit is the second.

## Should this conversation's context have lasted longer?

That's a separate question. Looking at this session honestly:

- I read multiple large files (resume.md is ~200 lines each invocation, run.log was 786KB at one point)
- The Red Team headless spawn produced a JSONL log I read significant chunks of
- Probe stderrs, state.json files, token-log.md — read repeatedly
- The CHANGELOG.md is 263k chars, I read 30+ lines from it

So this session's context window was loaded heavily by **my own diagnostic activity**, not because M55 didn't work. M55's brief generator would have helped *worker* spawns I didn't end up using — every direct claude -p I ran for probes bypassed the brief mechanism entirely.

## Why your plan usage burned through quickly today

Counting the real consumers of subscription budget today:
- 5 supervisor iters (Opus 4.7) — heaviest by far, $14.92 + $4.04 + smaller iters = ~$25
- Red Team headless (Opus 4.7) — $14.92
- ~7 probe spawns across v1/v3/v4 attempts — small but added up
- This conversation (Opus 4.7) — every turn reads accumulated context

Roughly **$50–70 of subscription quota in ~3 hours**. That's a lot. Claude Max 20× has a sliding 5-hour window — if the cap is something like $80–100/window equivalent, we'd plausibly hit it now.

## So what's the actual answer to your question?

**No, your context window didn't fail M55 — your subscription quota did.** Two distinct guard rails. M55's context-saving payoff would show up when:
1. You start a fresh M56 milestone using the substrate
2. Workers run with briefs instead of full repo re-reads
3. Your *per-milestone* budget drops 30–50% — meaning you can run more milestones per 5-hour window before hitting the cap

But today's work was the milestone that *built* the savings, not one that *used* them. Building the gym doesn't make you fitter; using it does. M56 is when you'd see the difference.

The honest read: today burned a lot of subscription budget on supervisor iters + Red Team + probes. That's the cost of measurement. From M56 onward, the same kind of work should consume noticeably less if the brief pattern + verify-gate are wired correctly. We won't really know until M56 runs.

How could 18 project already be current?

**Found the answer — and it's a real problem.** "Already up to date" is computed from these 7 mutators:

1. `updateProjectClaudeMd` — check if CLAUDE.md guard section needs updating
2. `createProjectChangelog` — check if CHANGELOG exists
3. `copyBinToolsToProject` — copy bin tools listed in `PROJECT_BIN_TOOLS`
4. `runProgressArchiveMigration` — one-time progress.md migration
5. `runTaskCounterRetirementMigration` — one-time migration
6. `ensureUnattendedConfig` — config existence check
7. `ensureUnattendedGitignore` — gitignore line check

**None of these reference the GSD-T version itself.** "Already up to date" doesn't mean "running v3.25.10" — it means "all 7 of these specific migration operations were no-ops."

So when update-all reported "18 already up to date":
- Their CLAUDE.md guards were already in place ✓
- Their CHANGELOG existed ✓
- The bin tools listed in `PROJECT_BIN_TOOLS` already existed at matching content ✓
- The historical migrations had already run on prior update-all invocations ✓

**But the M55 deliverables — `bin/cli-preflight.cjs`, `bin/parallel-cli.cjs`, `bin/gsd-t-context-brief.cjs`, `bin/gsd-t-verify-gate.cjs`, the new contracts — are NOT in `PROJECT_BIN_TOOLS`.** Look at that array: it stops at `headless-exit-codes.cjs` from old milestones.

So the projects that report "already up to date" **don't have the new M55 binaries**. The publish landed correctly on the npm package; the propagation just doesn't carry the new tools to project-local `bin/` directories.
